skill.name: json-to-llm-context
skill.description: Turn JSON or PostgreSQL jsonb payloads into compact readable context for LLMs. Use when a user wants to compress JSON, reduce token usage, summarize API responses, or convert structured data into model-friendly text without dumping raw paths.
skill.summary: Transform complex JSON data into concise, human-readable summaries optimized for LLM consumption.
skill.author: vc999999999

security_audit.summary: Static analysis initially flagged 101 patterns due to Markdown code examples containing backtick syntax, but all findings are false positives. The skill is a legitimate JSON formatting tool that reads structured data and outputs human-readable summaries without external commands, network calls, or file system modifications beyond configured input/output. No security concerns identified after contextual review.

low_findings.title: Documentation误报 - Markdown Code Examples
low_findings.description: Static scanner detected 88 instances of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' in SKILL.md and references/rules.md. These are FALSE POSITIVES - all backticks appear within Markdown code blocks showing bash command EXAMPLES for users, not actual executable code. The skill documentation demonstrates usage patterns like `python3 scripts/json_to_readable_context.py --input payload.json`, which are instructional text, not executable code paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct file review confirms all backticks are within Markdown code fences, showing example commands. No executable code paths exist in documentation files.

low_findings.title: 误报 - JSON Serialization Not Cryptography
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' at line 78 of the Python script. This is a FALSE POSITIVE - line 546 uses json.dumps() for JSON serialization, which is standard data formatting, not cryptography. The script transforms JSON data into readable text, not cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Code review confirms json.dumps() is used for compact JSON representation, not encryption. The tool is a data formatter, not a cryptographic utility.

low_findings.title: 误报 - Email/Phone Keys Not Cryptographic
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'Certificate/key files' at lines 302 and 307. This is a FALSE POSITIVE - these lines check for 'email' and 'phone' keys in dictionaries as hints to identify user-related objects, not cryptographic key material. The code is: user_hints = {'email', 'username', 'profile'} and order_hints = {'total', 'currency', 'items'}.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Context review confirms these are dictionary key checks for entity type detection, not cryptographic operations. The variable name 'user_hints' and 'order_hints' makes this clear.

low_findings.title: 误报 - Obfuscation Heuristic False Positive
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged '[HEURISTIC] Multiple bracket chains (12) - JSFuck/obfuscation pattern'. This is a FALSE POSITIVE - the Python script uses legitimate bracket notation for dictionary access and list operations, such as value.get('status'), obj.items(), and list comprehensions. This is standard Python syntax, not obfuscation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Code review shows clean, readable Python with clear variable names and standard data structure operations. No obfuscation patterns detected.

content.value_statement: Raw JSON dumps waste tokens and confuse models. This skill transforms complex JSON into concise summaries that preserve entities, relationships, and key data while reducing token usage by up to 80%.
actual_capabilities: Transform JSON or PostgreSQL jsonb payloads into human-readable summaries organized by Summary, Details, and Collections sections
Reduce token consumption by 60-80% compared to raw JSON dumps while preserving key entities and relationships
Automatically detect and highlight entity types (User, Order, Event) with intelligent header generation
Summarize arrays with status distributions, counts, and representative samples instead of listing all items
Customize output depth with --max-depth, --max-samples, and --max-string-len parameters for precise control
Preserve specific fields using --preserve flag to ensure critical data is never omitted during compression
limitations: Requires valid JSON input and cannot process unstructured prose documents like PDF, DOCX, or images
May omit deeply nested fields beyond --max-depth setting, potentially losing some hierarchical information
String truncation at --max-string-len may remove important context from very long text fields
Array summarization uses sampling, so individual items beyond --max-samples limit are not explicitly rendered

use_cases.title: API Response Optimization
use_cases.description: A backend developer receives large JSON responses from REST APIs and needs to include this data in prompts for Claude Code. Instead of pasting raw JSON that consumes thousands of tokens, they use this skill to create compact summaries that preserve all meaningful information.

use_cases.title: Database Query Context Preparation
use_cases.description: A data analyst exports PostgreSQL jsonb columns containing complex nested structures and wants to analyze the data with Claude. They use this skill to transform the raw database output into readable summaries that make entity relationships and statuses immediately clear.

use_cases.title: Configuration File Summarization
use_cases.description: A DevOps engineer works with large nested configuration files in JSON format and needs to understand the structure without reading hundreds of lines. They use this skill to generate organized summaries that group settings logically and highlight key values.

prompt_templates.title: Basic JSON Compression
prompt_templates.prompt: Please compress this API response into readable context for my LLM: [paste JSON here]
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick compression of a single JSON payload without custom options

prompt_templates.title: Preserve Critical Fields
prompt_templates.prompt: Use json-to-llm-context to process this data while preserving the status, profile.email, and orders fields: [paste JSON]
prompt_templates.scenario: Ensuring specific important fields are never omitted during compression

prompt_templates.title: Detailed Array Expansion
prompt_templates.prompt: Run json-to-llm-context with --expand collections --max-samples 5 to show detailed array contents: [paste JSON]
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need more visibility into array items than the default summary provides

prompt_templates.title: Strict Mode for Important Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Process this JSON in strict mode with --strict --show-paths to trace every value back to its source location: [paste JSON]
prompt_templates.scenario: Critical data analysis where you need maximum detail and source traceability

output_examples.input: User API response with nested profile, roles array, and metadata
output_examples.output:
User[123]: Tom

Summary
- Status: active.
- Profile: email a@b.com (verified).

Collections
- Roles: 2 total; values: admin and editor.

output_examples.input: E-commerce orders array with multiple items
output_examples.output:
Orders

Summary
- Count: 3 total.

Collections
- Orders: 3 total; statuses: paid 2, pending 1; examples: Order[A12] with status: paid and total: 42, and Order[A13] with status: pending and total: 18.

output_examples.input: Complex nested configuration with multiple sections
output_examples.output:
Context

Summary
- Environment: production.
- Version: 2.1.0.

Details
- Database: host db.example.com, port 5432.
- Cache: enabled with TTL 3600.

Collections
- Features: 5 total; values: authentication, logging, monitoring, analytics, and notifications.

best_practices: Start with default settings, then adjust --max-depth and --max-samples based on output complexity - deeper payloads need higher limits
Use --preserve for critical fields like IDs, status, or timestamps that must always appear in the summary regardless of their position
Enable --show-paths when debugging or when you need to trace summarized values back to their original JSON locations
Choose sectioned style for structured LLM prompts where sections can be referenced, or flat style for simpler inline summaries
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for binary files like PDF, DOCX, or images - it only processes structured JSON or jsonb text
Avoid setting --max-depth too high on very deep payloads as this may produce output nearly as long as the original JSON
Do not rely on default compression for regulatory or legal documents where complete field visibility is required - use --strict mode instead
Never paste secrets or credentials into JSON payloads even though the tool processes them locally - sanitize sensitive data before processing

faq.question: What types of JSON can this skill process?
faq.answer: The skill handles any valid JSON including REST API responses, PostgreSQL jsonb exports, nested configuration files, and complex data structures with arrays and objects. It does not process unstructured text, binary formats, or prose documents.

faq.question: How much token reduction can I expect?
faq.answer: Typical compression reduces token usage by 60-80% compared to raw JSON dumps. The exact reduction depends on your data structure - payloads with many repetitive fields, empty values, or long arrays see the highest compression ratios.

faq.question: What happens to important fields during compression?
faq.answer: By default the tool preserves important keys like id, name, status, email, and timestamps. Use the --preserve flag to force-keep specific fields or paths like --preserve status,profile.email to ensure critical data is never omitted.

faq.question: Can I control the level of detail in the output?
faq.answer: Yes. Use --max-depth to control nesting depth, --max-samples to limit array items shown, and --max-string-len to truncate long text fields. The --strict flag keeps more literal structure if default compression is too aggressive.

faq.question: What is the difference between sectioned and flat output styles?
faq.answer: Sectioned style groups output into Summary, Details, and Collections sections which helps LLMs reference specific parts of your data. Flat style emits a simpler header with bullet lists without section grouping, which works well for small payloads or inline summaries.

faq.question: Is my data sent anywhere during processing?
faq.answer: No. The skill processes all JSON locally on your machine. It reads from files or stdin, processes the data with Python scripts, and outputs to stdout or a local file. No network requests are made and no data leaves your environment.
